---
title: "Week 11 - Preparation: Computer set-up and data imporation"
subtitle: "CHC9012 | National Taiwan Normal University"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: serif
    transition: slide
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

## 🎯 Goals

**Prepare your computer:**

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
-   **Projects:** How to set-up a project in RStudio?
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
-   **Advanced scripts:** How to use Markdown documents to share better scripts?
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
-   **Data importation:** Where to find your PCIbex results file, and how to import it to RStudio?
:::

# 💻 Part 1: Prepare your computer: R projects

------------------------------------------------------------------------


## {.center}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" style="width: 60%; margin: auto;"}
### Why?

We want to be *organized* and **not messy**!
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
-   **Section 1.1:** Prepare the folder in your computer.
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
-   **Section 1.2:** Create the related R project.
:::

---


## 1.1 Setting up your folders {vertical-align="center"}


::: {.fragment}
1.  Navigate to your **`Documents`** (or **`文件`**) folder.
:::

::: {.fragment}
2.  Create your **Root Folder**: `CHC9012_Spring2025`
:::

::: {.fragment}
3.  Add the **Project Subfolders**:
    *   📁 `Slides`: Presentation files.
    *   📁 `PCIbex_design`: Scripts & materials.
    *   📁 `PreprocessingAnalysesR`: Datasets & R code.
:::


---

## You should obtain something like this {.center}

![](https://github.com/aymeric-collart/CHC9012/raw/main/course-materials/Images/clipboard-1590947435.png)


::: {.fragment}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
### Pay attention to the names

Generally, it is better not to use any space when naming folders and files!
:::

:::

---

## 1.2 Initializing Your R Project {vertical-align="center"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="1"}
**1. Launch `New Project Wizard`** <i class="fa-solid fa-square-plus" style="color: #2c7a7b;"></i>
<br><small>Click the **blue cube icon** in the top-left corner of RStudio.</small>
:::

<br>

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="2"}
**2. Select project type** <i style="color: #2c7a7b;"></i>
<br><small>Choose **"Existing Directory"** to link R to your folder structure.</small>
:::

<br>

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="3"}
**3. Finalize Connection** <i class="fa-solid fa-circle-check" style="color: #2c7a7b;"></i>
<br><small>Browse to `PreprocessingAnalysesR`, check **"Open in a new session"**, and click **Create Project**.</small>
:::
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.r-stack}
![](images/clipboard-2121617076.png){.fragment .fade-in-then-out data-fragment-index="1" width="100"}

![](images/clipboard-3751932394.png){.fragment .fade-in-then-out data-fragment-index="2" width="500"}

![](images/clipboard-1025670899.png){.fragment data-fragment-index="3" width="500"}
:::
:::

:::

::: {.fragment}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" .fragment data-fragment-index="3"}
**Benefit:** An `.Rproj` file sets your "Working Directory" automatically!
:::

:::

---

## Other advantages of creating projects {.center}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

1.  One RStudio window = One project
2.  Switch between them without worrying about the working directory of trying to find the right files!
3.  List of your projects on the top-right corner of RStudio. Here is mine for example:

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

![](images/clipboard-1750222853.png){width="400"}

:::

:::

---

## 📝 In-class exercise!
<br><br>
<center>**Task list:**

-   [ ] Find the R script from last week and move it to the `PreprocessingAnalysesR` folder.
-   [ ] Open the script within your newly created project.


# 📊 Part 2: Better and reproducible scripts with R Markdown


## From raw script to functional: R Markdown {.center}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="1"}
**1. Create the file** 📄
<br><small>Go to **New File > R Markdown**. This moves you from raw code to a "living" document.</small>
:::

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="2"}
**2. Configure (YAML)** ⚙️
<br><small>The header between `---` controls your title, author, and output format (HTML/PDF).</small>
:::

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="3"}
**3. Text + Code chunks** 📝
<br><small>Write text normally; insert **Code Chunks** (`{r}`) for analysis.</small>
:::

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="4"}
**4. Run your codes** ▶️
<br><small>Use the green **Play Button** to test individual steps.</small>
:::

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="5"}
**5. The Final "Knit"** 🧶
<br><small>Click **Knit** to stitch everything into a polished report automatically!</small>
:::
:::

::: {.column width = "45%"}
::: {.r-stack}
![](https://github.com/aymeric-collart/CHC9012/raw/main/course-materials/Images/ScriptTypes.png){.fragment .fade-in-then-out data-fragment-index="1"}

![](https://github.com/aymeric-collart/CHC9012/raw/main/course-materials/Images/Markdown_YAML.png){.fragment .fade-in-then-out data-fragment-index="2" style="width: 100%; height: auto; border-radius: 8px;"}

![](https://github.com/aymeric-collart/CHC9012/raw/main/course-materials/Images/RCodeBlock_WarningMessage.png){.fragment .fade-in-then-out data-fragment-index="3" style="width: 100%; height: auto; border-radius: 8px;"}

![](https://github.com/aymeric-collart/CHC9012/raw/main/course-materials/Images/RCodeBlock_RunBlock.png){.fragment data-fragment-index="4" style="width: 100%; height: auto; border-radius: 8px;"}
:::
:::

:::

---

## 📝 In-class exercise!
<br><br>
<center>**Task list:**

-   [ ] Create a new Markdown document, and call it `ScriptWeek10_Markdown`.
-   [ ] Use the script from last week, but adapt it to the Markdown format. Be creative!


# 📊 Part 3: Download your PCIbex results and import them into RStudio

## Fake experiment for this class

<a href="https://farm.pcibex.net/p/qCbaTy/" class="btn btn-outline-primary btn-sm"> <i class="bi bi-download"></i> Try the experiment </a>

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="The code of the fake experiment is given on the website"}

:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
### Fake results!

An AI robot did the experiment several times. I also pretended that I was a "bad" participant several times. The results are just to show you how to analyze data from PCIbex, they do not have other value!
:::

---

::: panel-tabset
## 3.1. Download the results file

-   On the PCIbex platform, click on the `Results` button.

-   Then, click on `Download`.

![](images/clipboard-1787923676.png)

-   Finally, place the file in the folder you just created on your computer!

## 3.2. Importing the data into RStudio

-   [ ] First, open a new Markdown document. Call it `DataImportFromPCIbex`.

-   [ ] Second, create an `r` block, paste the following code, and run it to activate the function.

``` diff
read.pcibex <- function(filepath, auto.colnames=TRUE, fun.col=function(col,cols){cols[cols==col]<-paste(col,"Ibex",sep=".");return(cols)}) {
  n.cols <- max(count.fields(filepath,sep=",",quote=NULL),na.rm=TRUE)
  if (auto.colnames){
    cols <- c()
    con <- file(filepath, "r")
    while ( TRUE ) {
      line <- readLines(con, n = 1, warn=FALSE)
      if ( length(line) == 0) {
        break
      }
      m <- regmatches(line,regexec("^# (\\d+)\\. (.+)\\.$",line))[[1]]
      if (length(m) == 3) {
        index <- as.numeric(m[2])
        value <- m[3]
        if (is.function(fun.col)){
         cols <- fun.col(value,cols)
        }
        cols[index] <- value
        if (index == n.cols){
          break
        }
      }
    }
    close(con)
    return(read.csv(filepath, comment.char="#", header=FALSE, col.names=cols))
  }
  else{
    return(read.csv(filepath, comment.char="#", header=FALSE, col.names=seq(1:n.cols)))
  }
}
```

-   [ ] Third, import the data using this function. The code is as below. We store the data into a new dataset (variable) that we call `data`.

``` diff
data <- read.pcibex("results_FAKE_FOR_CHC9012.csv")
```

-   [ ] Finally, it is always a good idea to have a look at the data that you just imported. You can do that through these lines of code.

``` diff
head(data, 20)

str(data)

summary(data)
```
:::

---

## 📝 In-class exercise!
<br><br>
<center>**Task list:**

-   [ ] Look at the whole table. To do so, click on the variable, or just write `view(data)` in the console
-   [ ] Manually, identify and take notes of (1) what information is missing, (2) which rows should be erased/kept, and (3) which columns should be deleted or added.

---

::: {.callout-important icon="true"}
## 📝 Assignment for next week

No assignment for next week! Please carefully review the content of these past two weeks, and finalize the data collection of your own project.
:::
